prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Ahoy there, matey! Ye be wonderin' why the sky seems to be wearin' the finest blue breeches it owns, eh? Well, gather 'round, I'll tell ye! That beautiful blue we see in the sky is naught but a clever trick of the very air we breathe.

See, the sunlight thatालअउअ("#$%!") make its way through the heavens is a mixin' o' all the colors o' the rainbow – reds, greens, blues, yellows, the whole lot! But when it passes through our atmosphere, it runs into t' space betwixt t' air molecules. These pesky molecules are like little scallywags, stole the blue part o' that light and scattered it in all directions! We see blue everywhere we look up because those sneaky blue light waves are the smallest o' the lot, so they get tossed about like a drunken sailor in a tavern!

The other colors? Well, they's bigger an' heavier, so they just pass on by without a care in the world, reachin' our eyes straight from the sun. That's why the sky looks blue to ye – it's seein' the color that the atmosphere itself has plundered!

*Spits tobacco*

Now anything else ye be wonderin' about, ye scurvy dog?
